schools.summary: Ellicott: Mark gives the question in the singular with the emphatic name; Robertson: 'Simon,' the old name, his boasted loyalty already failing — Christ knows the weakness of the flesh.
Wesley: addressed to 'the zealous, the confident Peter,' who had vowed never to be offended.
Gill: the temptation is to deny Christ when they see him taken; watching and praying the defence.
Barnes connects spirit-ready/flesh-weak to the divided self (Romans 7:18-25; Galatians 5:17) — a warning and a charitable excuse.
Gill: he returned to the same place and prayed to the same purport — the repetition marking the depth of the agony, not eased at the first asking.

Barnes: they could find no answer, conscious of the impropriety; Robertson on the vivid 'weighed down' eyes and the speechlessness (Mark alone), recalling their like embarrassment at the Transfiguration (Mark 9:6).

Ellicott: Mark omits the third prayer; 'it is enough' (apechei) peculiar to Mark, the transition from 'sleep on now' to 'the hour is come'; Robertson on the papyri 'paid in full' / ironical sense; Barnes: 'All is over.'
Cyril: he came to his passion willingly, lest the world perish in its sins.

sides.position: No need of further reproof — the enemy is at the gate
'All is over' — the time to help is past

schools.summary: Gill: 'let us go' to meet the enemy, for there is no escaping — Christ rises toward the betrayer, not away.
The calm announcement of the betrayer at hand is the word of one who has accepted his hour and advances to embrace it — the voluntary passion.
Bede: Christ tranquil goes to his persecutors; Judas keeps the shame of a disciple, betraying by a kiss; foolish to seek with swords one who offers himself, taught daily in the temple.
The aggravation of 'one of the twelve'; the armed multitude from the whole Jewish authority against One who offered no resistance.

Robertson on the 'token' (syssēmon, Mark only) and 'lead him away safely' (Mark only) — Judas wished no slip; the kiss a desecration of friendly greeting; Barnes on the treacherous kiss (2 Samuel 20:9; Psalm 55:21).
Wesley: Christ had perhaps permitted the disciples this kiss on returning; Judas turned the token of affection into the badge of betrayal.
Ellicott: the MSS. give the Hebrew 'Rabbi, Rabbi'; Barnes hears the false zeal of 'Master, master'; the haste ('straightway') and the kiss compress the betrayal into feigned affection.
Gill: the seizure followed Christ's words to Judas (Matthew's 'Friend,' Luke's 'betrayest thou with a kiss?'), which Mark omits.
Mark's bare brevity — 'they took him' — the willing Lamb seized, omitting the falling-back (John 18:6) the other Gospels supply.
Robertson: Mark does not name Peter (only John, after Peter's death); Peter aimed to kill Malchus; Mark omits Christ's rebuke (Matthew 26:52); a flash of carnal zeal against the willing Passion.
Ellicott: 'as against a robber'; Robertson on the irony that Jesus would hang between two robbers in Barabbas's place; the armed midnight seizure absurd against One who offered no resistance.
Ellicott: 'ye seized me not'; Gill: daily in the temple since the entry; Barnes on the Scriptures fulfilled (Isaiah 53; Luke 24:44) — exposing their cowardice and naming the divine necessity.
Gill: the disciples, not the multitude, forsook him; Barnes: the fulfilment of 'all ye shall be offended' (Mark 14:27) and the forsaken sufferer (Psalm 88:8) — the complete desertion.
Barnes/Gill survey the guesses (John, James, a household youth, a neighbor); recorded by Mark alone, an eyewitness detail; Lightfoot on the sindōn (linen garment of the Law).
Bede reads the flight allegorically and notes his 'long sojourn in the flesh' — a hint at the tradition identifying him with Mark.

debates.question: Who was the young man who fled naked?

sides.position: A neighbor or household youth roused by the noise

schools.summary: Gill: like Joseph leaving his garment (Genesis 39); Clarke on the Eastern custom of slipping loose clothing to flee; Robertson on the sindōn (linen, used for the dead).
Bede: he fled from the wicked crowd but not in heart from the Lord, his love remaining though his body fled.

Bede: Caiaphas (who bought his priesthood) and the whole council; following 'afar off' the seed of denial; the fire allegorised as charity vs covetousness (Luke 12:49; Hosea 7:4; Matthew 24:12); Pseudo-Jerome: the hall the world, the servants devils.

outliers.note: Reads Peter's following afar off and warming at the enemies' fire as the predisposing causes of his denial.

schools.summary: Robertson: Peter following 'afar off,' neither openly with Christ nor safely away, at home among the officers, the firelight shining in his face — not hidden as he supposed; Ellicott/Clarke on 'at the light' exposing him to the maid.
Wesley: an amazing proof of providence that, with all their authority and rewards, no two consistent witnesses could charge him with any crime — a divine attestation of his innocence.
Gill: the chief priests most busy in seeking witness to a verdict already fixed — 'to put him to death.'

Robertson: the testimonies 'not equal,' repeated false witness (imperfects), no two agreeing for a capital sentence (Deuteronomy 19:15); Lightfoot: the rabbinic law of agreeing witnesses, which honest scrutiny would have used to expose the perjury.
Wesley: 'agreed not together' = not equal to a capital charge.

Robertson: a desperate fresh attempt (conative imperfect) with the temple-charge; Ellicott: Mark less precise than Matthew (who shows the two-witness rule barely met); Gill: witnesses stood to testify.

Robertson: the 'made with/without hands' antithesis is Mark's alone, a perversion of John 2:19 (the temple of his body); Swete: Jesus did destroy the old order and raise the new; Ellicott echoes Hebrews 9:11.
Wesley: they reached back three years (John 2:19) for matter — a silent attestation of his blameless ministry; Bede: a false witness twists words to a sense not spoken.
Ellicott: peculiar to Mark, the temple-charge too failed to agree (in details of time/place or the words); Gill: they agreed in substance but not 'equally,' not enough for a capital sentence.

themes: neither so did their witness agree

schools.summary: Bede: the high priest rises in anger to provoke an answer for accusation, his bodily motion showing his madness; Pseudo-Jerome: Christ's silence fulfils Isaiah 53:7, atoning for Adam's excuses (Genesis 3:10).
The deliberate silence — the contradictory charges unworthy of answer, and to answer would feed the high priest's design.

Robertson: Mark adds 'answered nothing' to 'kept silent,' omitting Matthew's oath; Ellicott: 'the Son of the Blessed' = a reverent Jewish name for God (Matthew/Luke 'Son of God'); Barnes on the silence as prophecy (Isaiah 53:7).

Robertson: Mark's 'I am' (egō eimi) definite beyond controversy, more direct than Matthew's 'Thou hast said'; the claim of Daniel 7:13 — session at God's right hand and coming on the clouds; Cyril gathers the apostolic 'right hand' texts.

themes: the Son of man at the right hand

schools.summary: Ellicott: Mark uses the word for the inner garment; Gill: rending clothes the customary sign of supposed blasphemy; the confession seized as the charge — 'what need we witnesses?'
Bede (v.60): the rent priestly robe a figure of the Jewish priesthood torn away for the priests' wickedness.

Ellicott: 'guilty of death' = liable to the death-penalty (older usage); Robertson: 'they all' implies Joseph and Nicodemus absent, the hostile majority unanimous; condemned on his own confession, not any proven crime.

Ellicott: the covering of the face (Mark/Luke) points the taunt 'Prophesy'; Robertson on the veil, the jeer, and the slaps (rapismata); Gill: the spitting and smiting fulfil Isaiah 50:6; 1:6 — and the mocked Prophet had foretold this very scene.

Augustine harmonises the order of the four accounts and the denials (different maids, the second within at the fire, the return omitted for brevity); Bede: recognized first by a woman (sin and redemption of that sex); to deny Christ is also to deny oneself a Christian.
The denial opens; Peter recognized by a maidservant, the weakest of accusers.

debates.question: How are the four accounts of the denials reconciled?

sides.position: Complementary, not contradictory — different maids and persons, the return omitted for brevity

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'And thou also wast with the Nazarene, Jesus' spoken with emphatic scorn; Robertson: Peter twice at the fire, 'the Nazarene' (Matthew: 'Galilean'), several speaking; the firelight betrayed him.

Robertson: the denial fuller in Mark, the evasive 'I neither know nor understand'; the 'porch' (proaulion, NT hapax); 'the cock crew' textually doubtful here (genuine in v.72), Mark alone giving the twofold crowing (Mark 14:30).

themes: I know not what thou sayest

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'the maid' who had pointed him out before (or another, Gill); Robertson: the charge now 'to them that stood by' — 'This is one of them' — overheard by Peter, the accusations multiplying (Augustine: two voices).
Barnes on the Galilean accent (Acts 2:7) betraying him; Ellicott: 'thy speech agreeth thereto' wanting in many MSS., perhaps Mark's own later gloss; the second denial and the mounting third charge.
Robertson on 'curse' (anathematizein) and Matthew's word for calling down curses on oneself; Gill: he wished dreadful things on himself and swore — the third denial, disowning the Lord as 'this man.'

Gill: the second cock-crow about 3 a.m.; Mark alone gives the twofold crowing, fulfilling the word exactly; Clarke/Ellicott weigh the obscure epibalōn ('he fell a-weeping' / covered his head / rushed out).
Bede (v.66): Peter washed away his denial with the tears of penitence — the contrast with despairing Judas, the model of the fallen disciple restored.

debates.question: What does 'when he thought thereon' (epibalōn) mean?

sides.position: Casting his mind on it — reflecting
Covering his head, or rushing out, in anguish

schools.summary: Pascha = 'passing over' (Exod 12:13), not Passion; Pseudo-Jerome heaps up figures of the slain Lamb as the Evangelist prepares to tell of Christ's death.
Theophylact: Christ himself appointed the day, willing to be crucified on the Passover because he is the true Passover.
Robertson dates it Tuesday evening, harmonising Mark/Matthew/John; 'by craft' = a secret plan to seize him without rousing the people.
They had first planned to kill him at the feast (John 11:57) but, seeing his powerful following after the Entry and temple debate, resolved to wait till it was over.
Gill: with the pilgrim crowds gathering, they would not seize him then for fear of an uproar.
Allegory: Bethany the house of obedience, Simon the converted world, the woman the Church's faith and her precious nard; Augustine holds Luke's sinner and John's Mary one woman who anointed twice.
Theophylact distinguishes two or three women across the Gospels, John's Mary of Bethany from Luke's sinner.
Liquid perfume of 'pistic' (genuine) Indian nard (cf John 12:3); Robertson holds this distinct from Luke 7:36; debate over the alabaster flask and its costly balsam.

debates.question: How many anointing women do the Gospels record?

sides.position: Luke's sinner and John's Mary are one, anointing twice
this is distinct from Luke 7's anointing

schools.summary: Mark's 'some' is an intermediate stage between Matthew's 'the disciples' and John's naming of Judas as the murmurer.
Gill (with Wesley): the indignation kindled by Judas, who incited the rest; the complaint spoken 'to the women.'

The 300 pence reckoned at near a year's wage (cf John 12:5, common to Mark and John); Pliny on nard's price; 'murmured' (enebrimonto) the snorting of horses, perhaps from Peter's memory of Judas (John 12:4).
The 'good' work is 'noble,' the higher goodness — a usage peculiar to Mark; Christ defends her against the murmur.
Gill: knowing their secret indignation and continual teasing, Jesus bids them cease to chide her.
Grounded in Deut 15:11 ('the poor never cease'); Mark alone adds 'whensoever ye will ye may do them good' — charity is constant, his bodily presence passing.
'What she had she did'; Mary, unable to comprehend his death, yet showed sympathy and some understanding none of her critics had; 'to the burying' = an anticipated embalming (Matt 26:12).
A foresight of the worldwide gospel wedded to her memorial; Robertson: mausoleums crumble, but this monument still fills the world with fragrance.
Cyril turns to the eternal life the preached gospel offers by faith.

themes: the gospel in all the world

schools.summary: Judas seeks to recoup the 'lost' ointment; 'he went out' shows free, spontaneous wickedness, not compulsion; one of the Twelve 'in number, not in merit'; Satan entered him.
Chrysostom: the Evangelist names his town to distinguish him but withholds 'traitor,' teaching us to shun detraction.
Robertson stresses 'the one of the twelve' who did it; Wesley dates the bargain to just after the reproof, anger now added to greed.
The rabbis welcomed the treachery as providential; 'conveniently' was the point — Judas could take him away from the crowds (Luke 22:6); Mark, like Luke, omits the sum.
Clarke: joy at the chance to murder an innocent man is 'completely infernal.'

The 14th, leaven put away, the lamb slain at even (1 Cor 5:7); that night the old Passover yields to the Mysteries of his Body; Theophylact infers Christ had no dwelling and could have avoided his Passion.
The lamb slain toward evening (Exod 12:6), preparations on the fourteenth; the disputed harmony of the day; the man with the pitcher known only by Christ's foreknowledge.
Henry: no apprehension of trouble should put us out of frame for holy ordinances — Christ keeps the feast with the cross in view.
The number (two) from Mark, the names (Peter and John) from Luke 22:8; the water-pitcher sign common to Mark and Luke; Clarke: Christ's exact foreknowledge honours even mean, honest labour.
Wesley: a seasonable proof of his knowing all things and his influence over men's minds, to steady their faith.
'Goodman' = master of the house; 'guest-chamber' (katalyma) the word for 'inn' (Luke 2:7); the better reading 'my guest-chamber' implies the owner's discipleship; an Eastern strangers' room.
'Furnished and prepared' = spread with carpets/couches and specially arranged for the Paschal Supper; an upstairs room (anagaion) where Jesus keeps the feast with the Twelve alone, reclining.
Gill: Peter and John went directly from Bethany and found all as Christ foretold.
Barnes: 'found as he had said' — the Lord's word does not fail (John 16:4).

Christ foretells the traitor to give him room for repentance, neither naming him openly (lest he grow shameless) nor wholly silent (lest he hasten the deed); Theophylact: they reclined after fulfilling the legal Passover.
Robertson: the dramatic present 'cometh'; the regular passover meal at 6 P.M.; Gill on the company assembling at evening.
'Reclined,' not sat; 'he that eateth with me' (Ps 41:9) — the betrayal of a table-companion is monstrous even by Eastern hospitality; the words wound every conscience yet leave the traitor veiled.
Cyril, on fidelity and reward, sets the faithful against the faithless.

themes: one of you shall betray me

schools.summary: Gill: all but Judas grew sorrowful at the saying.
Clarke notes the textual uncertainty of the closing 'and another said, Is it I?'
One of the Twelve dipping in the dish — the sign escaped the others; Jesus gave the sop to Judas, who knew he was known (Matt 26:21).
Clarke: Easterners shared one dish only in close attachment, so dipping with the Lord marked an intimate — deepening the treachery.
'Goeth' = to die, as written (Matt 26:24); the woe links to offences (Matt 18:7); Mark omits the Traitor's own 'Is it I?'
Cyril: he came to his passion willingly, lest the world perish in its sins.

debates.question: Does the decree of Scripture lessen Judas's guilt?

sides.position: No — the death was foreordained and freely embraced, yet the betrayer is fully guilty

themes: the Son of man goeth as written

schools.summary: Sacramental realism: Theophylact — the bread changed into the very Body, veiled for our weakness; Chrysostom — Christ, not man, consecrates by 'This is My Body'; Cyril — communicants made of one body and blood with Christ.
Bede sets it under the Melchizedek priesthood (Ps 110:4); Gregory on his giving thanks while bearing others' stripes; Pseudo-Jerome a 'mystical' transfiguration, the Body also the suffering Church.
Henry: instituted as a memorial of Christ's death, food for the soul, till his second coming.
Barnes/Gill read the elements as sign and emblem (John 6:48-58; 1 Cor 11:23).

debates.question: In what sense is the bread Christ's body?

sides.position: changed into / a true participation in the very Body
a sign and memorial sealing union by faith

schools.summary: 'Blessing' of the bread, 'giving thanks' of the cup; the cup the country's wine mixed with water, here 'the fruit of the vine'; Mark and Matthew agree with Paul (1 Cor 11:23).
Cyril: by these mysteries the faithful are made one body and blood with Christ.
'Is being shed' (present participle); the cup of communion in his blood (1 Cor 10:16); 'new testament' = the new covenant in blood.
Calvin: the words direct us to the sacrifice of his death, without which the Supper is ill kept; 'many' means not a part of the world only.
Wesley: a perpetual sign of his blood shed to establish the new covenant for all who believe.
'The product of the vine'; Mark's 'kingdom of God' vs Matthew's 'of My Father'; the 'new' drinking pictured in the prophets' kingdom-abundance (Joel 3:18; Amos 9:13).
Wesley: no more wine before he dies; the next will be heavenly, not earthly.

Thanks after as before the meal; the hymn the praise of the Psalms (Ps 22:26); Bede recalls John 17 and reads the going to Olivet as rising higher through the Sacraments; Augustine on Peter's denial, told most minutely by Mark.
The hymn is the Hallel (Ps 113-118) sung at Passover.
Calvin: the Synoptics omit the discourses of John 14-17, noting only that Christ went out of his own accord to meet his enemies.
The citation of Zech 13:7 — the smitten shepherd, the scattered sheep; Wesley on 'this night' (the day reckoned from evening, Gen 1:5), sharpening the nearness of the prediction.
Gill: spoken as they went to the Mount of Olives.
Gill: Christ assures them of his rising for their comfort, foreseeing their discouragement at his seizing and crucifixion.
Barnes: the risen Lord did go before them into Galilee (Mark 16:7) — the promise a consolation and a tryst.
Peter's boast of loyalty even if all desert (told in all the Gospels, cf Matt 26:33).
Gill: greatly moved, Peter protests his own constancy above the rest.
The 'twice' is Mark's own detail (agreeing with the 'second time' of 14:72), the cock-crowing marking the third watch (Mark 13:35); every word emphatic — day, night, number.
Cyril recalls the like sovereign 'Verily I say unto thee' to the penitent thief.
'Exceeding vehemently' (ekperissos, only in Mark, preserving Peter's own word); the tense implies repeated, continuous protest; all the disciples joined the vow.
Gill: he spoke louder, with more eagerness, in a more peremptory, self-confident way.

Gethsemane 'the valley of fatness' — praying in the valley keeps humility and interior love; Theophylact: his custom of solitary prayer, the three taken to see his sufferings and know he is truly man; Augustine guards 'if it be possible.'
'Sit here till I have prayed'; Henry: he begins with the sorest sufferings, those of the soul, freely admitted.

Mark's stronger words: 'sore amazed' (ekthambeisthai, also 9:15) — Jesus feels amazement facing the struggle; Wesley: shocking amazement mingled with grief, sorrow on every side; the three who saw his glory now see his anguish.
Barnes links it to 'now is my soul troubled' (John 12:27), here deepened 'unto death.'
Gill: spoken to the three drawn apart to share his vigil.
Descriptive imperfects 'fell... prayed'; 'the hour' long foreseen (John 12:27) now dreaded — a human trait all understand; the prayer 'with strong crying and tears' (Heb 5:7).
Gill: he went forward about a stone's cast and fell to the ground.
'Abba, Father' — both Aramaic and Greek, not translation but both terms (Gal 4:6; impress in Rom 8:15), peculiar to Mark; denotes filial tenderness; he was heard (Heb 5:7) and helped to submit.
Wesley: Mark adds 'Father' to explain the Aramaic 'Abba.'
